artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Just catching my breath after another long day and wanted to update you on a few things before I forget the details.

I spent part of the afternoon in the garage working on “Grandma Helen’s Garden Reassembled.” I’m still using the floral china from her 1950s dinner set that we packed up from her Lemont apartment after she passed in March 2023. Every time I glue down another shard I can picture us in that kitchen with the boxes spread out. The backing is still that reclaimed barn wood Dad saved from the old corn crib on your Lockport property, and I finally worked in more of the copper wiring I stripped from the table lamp I accidentally broke during that awful panic attack in my Oakwood School classroom back in November 2022. It’s strange how those two moments—losing Grandma and falling apart in front of those seventh graders—are literally wired together in this piece.

School itself is the usual chaos: grading, art club, kids forgetting to rinse brushes. Sofia’s piano teacher moved her lesson to Thursdays, so Miguel’s on soccer duty with Lucas now. We’re still debating whether we can afford to replace the car before winter; the rattling gets worse every week.

I’m thinking of bringing “River of Receipts” over on Sunday so you and Dad can see it again—it’s the one stitched from grocery and hardware receipts from around Lemont and Lockport, 2017 through this year. It looks even more depressing now that eggs are a small fortune.

We’ll be at your place around 10 on Sunday for coffee, like usual. Let me know if you need anything from Target or Aldi.

Love you,
Clara

Studio notebook – 2025-02-02, late night after kids’ bedtime

Trying to make sense of how much of our family’s medical and school paperwork has quietly seeped into the work.

For the new large collage (working title: “Ledger of Small Emergencies”), I’ve been shredding and hand-stitching some of Mom’s Silver Cross Hospital discharge instructions and pharmacy leaflets from her blood pressure and diabetes meds—those stacks we’ve been collecting since her ER visit for hypertension and dizziness back in March 2022 through the follow-ups in November 2023 at Silver Cross in New Lenox. I’m pairing them with copies of the CVS receipts from the Lockport pharmacy and the cardboard backer from the box her glucose monitor came in. It feels like a private medical archive sewn into the background.

On top of that layer I’m testing small panels made from Sofia’s Spring 2023 River Valley School IEP pages—color copies of the reading intervention reports and Dr. Lane’s assessment summary, plus those printed emails from her special ed case manager about her anxiety episodes during state testing. I’m mounting them on the cardboard from her Chromebook shipping box. It’s a lot of vulnerability in one piece, even if most viewers won’t read the tiny text.

Need to think carefully about titles and wall text so I’m not exposing Mom’s diagnoses or Sofia’s anxiety to random strangers, but I also don’t want to sanitize what living in this paper trail actually feels like.

Reminder: buy more neutral thread; the red reads too melodramatic over hospital forms.

Hey Ana,

Typing this from the kitchen while the dishwasher hums and Lucas sings some nonsense soccer chant to himself upstairs.

Dad’s been on my mind a lot since his minor stroke in Lockport last November. Seeing him with the walker at Christmas was rough. I emailed the Lemont Center for the Arts about the upcoming show and asked if they can install “River of Receipts” in a quieter corner so they can put a chair about 4–5 feet in front of it. That way Dad can sit directly in front of the piece with his walker parked next to him without blocking traffic. I suggested the corner near the south wall, close to the emergency exit but not right in front of it.

At the same time, I told them I’d feel better if my work isn’t on the wall that’s visible from the big front windows. Ever since the break-in at 418 Willow Bridge Lane on February 9, 2024—when someone smashed the living room window and stole my laptop with all the District 113A student grades on it—I get jumpy if I feel like I’m “on display” from the street. The idea of standing in front of my own piece and seeing people stare in from outside makes my chest tighten.

Anyway, enough heavy stuff. How are your patients? Still getting people who refuse to floss but want whitening miracles?

We’re planning to come to Lockport on Sunday like usual. I’ll bring pan dulce if you grab coffee from that place on 9th Street Dad likes.

Love,
C

metadata.subject: Dad, the show, and Sunday plans

artifacts.content:
Event: Install day – “Grandma Helen’s Garden Reassembled” & “River of Receipts”
Date: 2025-03-07 (Friday)
Time: 2:00–3:00 p.m.
Location: Lemont Center for the Arts – main gallery
Attendees: Clara, gallery techs (max 2)

Notes:

– Because of the panic attack I had during class at Oakwood School in March 2024 and the reduced teaching schedule Lemont-Bromberek put me on afterward, I need this to be a short, calm install. Ask them again to keep it to about 30–45 minutes, with no more than two staff in the room and no loud ladder-clanking chaos.

– Back is still finicky since the January 2024 car accident on Archer Avenue in Lemont. I really shouldn’t be lifting more than 10–15 lbs, so remind myself: NO ladders, NO trying to be helpful with the 25 lb frame for “Grandma Helen’s Garden Reassembled.” Let the techs handle mounting into the wall studs.

– Double-check placement for “River of Receipts”: ask for a quieter corner near the south wall so Dad can sit with his walker (chair about 4–5 feet from the piece), and make sure it’s not directly visible from the front windows. Still too much anxiety/insomnia from the 2/9/24 break-in at 418 Willow Bridge Lane and the stolen laptop with District 113A grades.

– Leave by 3:15 to pick up Lucas from Oakwood and Sofia from River Valley.

metadata.title: Install – Lemont Center for the Arts

artifacts.content:
Thread with Miguel – 2025-04-04 evening

Clara: You alive? How did the day go?

Miguel: Barely. Long meeting with HR about last July’s safety violation again. Probation review.

Clara: Ugh. I’ve been thinking about that with the show. I told the Lemont Center curator not to install “River of Receipts” and the reclaimed concrete fragments anywhere near the main photo wall or signage that mentions specific bridge or road projects. I don’t want you getting tagged in some Instagram shot looking like you’re posing with job-site debris from your Chicago infrastructure firm.

Miguel: Thank you. HR would lose it.

Clara: Also, reminder: Sofia’s River Valley School field trip to the Lemont Center is on May 18, 2025. Dr. Patel at Lemont Family Practice said her dust-mite and mold allergies (diagnosed back in March 2024) mean we need to be extra careful. She’ll be in the gallery for like 3–4 hours. I already asked them to avoid any reclaimed insulation or funky carpet padding near my pieces—don’t want a repeat of the reaction she had at the Chicago Children’s Museum last October when we almost ended up in the ER.

Miguel: Good call. I’ll keep my inhaler lecture ready.

Clara: Ha. Also, can you swap soccer pickup with me that day? I’ll probably be wiped after herding fourth graders around art.

Miguel: Deal. I’ll bring Lucas to practice and you can collapse on the couch.

Clara: Perfect marriage.

Hi Diego,

I was sorting through boxes in the garage tonight and found the old folder with my disciplinary write-up from May 2022—the one Principal Harris at Lemont-Bromberek gave me for talking about climate change protests in my 7th-grade art class at Old Quarry. Remember how my LBS #604 union rep helped me file that three-page grievance form, and we sat in that stuffy conference room while they read the district social media policy at me like scripture?

Anyway, I scanned everything, tore it into strips, and stitched it into the background of a new panel that’s going to hang near “River of Receipts.” It’s layered with photocopies of our overdue ComEd and Nicor utility bills from the Archer Avenue apartment days (2020–2023), plus those debt collection letters Mr. Patel’s office sent after he hired that agency in February 2021. I even flattened a couple of the late-rent reminder envelopes and eviction notice slips and used them as the backing. It’s like a greatest-hits collage of being scolded by both the district and capitalism.

On the surface it just looks like abstract text and numbers, but if you get close you can read “FINAL SHUT-OFF NOTICE” and “Written Warning” peeking through the paint. Feels good to literally sew that whole mess into something I control.

On a more normal note: are you still coming Sunday for coffee at Mom and Dad’s? Sofia wants to show you her latest comic, and Lucas insists he can beat you at Mario Kart now.

Love,
Clara

Personal journal – 2025-03-18, 11:40 p.m.

Today was one of those days that shows up in the work whether I want it to or not.

Morning: sat in yet another meeting at Oakwood about Lucas. We’re heading into his April 3, 2025 IEP meeting to talk about his sensory processing issues and how he tends to bolt when he’s overstimulated. I told the team I’m nervous about the Lemont Center opening—crowds, glass doors, him running toward the exits. I’ve already asked the curator to install “River of Receipts” at least 15–20 feet from any high-traffic doors so Mom can hang back with him in a safer corner.

Afternoon: checked in on Mom’s knee—still sore from the November 2024 replacement at Silver Cross, but she’s proud of hitting her PT goals. I keep scheduling art stuff so I can get back to Lockport the same day; I can’t leave her meds and exercises to chance yet.

I keep thinking about how many bodies are woven into these pieces: Grandma Helen’s hospice months in Lemont, Mom’s blood pressure and diabetes, Dad’s stroke, Sofia’s reading interventions and testing anxiety, Lucas’s bolting. Even Miguel’s overnight emergency inspection on that collapsed bridge in downtown Chicago back in January 2024, when I had that 3 a.m. anxiety episode and knocked over the bedside lamp—I stripped the copper from that lamp and threaded it through the mosaic with Grandma’s china and the barn wood from the Lockport corn crib.

Maybe this is just what my work is now: a family medical chart and incident report, disguised as mixed-media.

metadata.title: Journal – family woven into the work
